title: Jack and His Stepdame
summary: CME00073 is the repository XML for a University of Michigan manually keyed and proofread electronic text derived from the edited-text portion, printed pp. 66–82, of Julius Zupitza's 1893 article 'Jak and his step dame.' Zupitza transcribed Oxford, Bodleian Libraries, MS. Rawl. C. 86, fols. 52r–59r (R), and recorded variants from the former Porkington manuscript (P), now National Library of Wales, Brogyntyn MS II.1, fols. 139r–150r. The P evidence is mediated: Zupitza explicitly says he knew P only through J. O. Halliwell's 1855 transcription on pp. 46–62 of Early English Miscellanies. Complete public provider PDFs of the two printed source volumes and complete provider-published IIIF sequences for both manuscript witnesses are cached locally; each manuscript bundle retains the whole published sequence and identifies the work's folio and canvas range inside it.
review_notes: The immediate lineage is not Zupitza directly to both manuscripts: R was inspected directly, while P was available to Zupitza only through Halliwell's printed transcription.

The XML contains 73 top-level edited stanzas and 438 top-level verse lines. Its apparent totals of 74 LG and 450 L elements include a twelve-line P-only alternative ending nested inside the note to the final R stanza.

This manifest traces the sources materially represented in CME00073. The poem has a much broader manuscript and early-print tradition that is described by modern scholarship but is not silently promoted into the immediate derivation graph.
The U-M legacy inventory dates the edition to 1895, but the journal title page, article, U-M item record, and modern bibliography establish volume 90 as 1893.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00073
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion and publication workflow, supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: Internal identifiers IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID are all CME00073.
The 2016-09-16 revision entry records structural review, proof-sheet checking, page-break verification, and corrections to milestones, line groups, and headings.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME item CME00073
entities.description: A public manually keyed and proofread electronic text selecting Zupitza's edited poem, stanza headings, manuscript and comparative milestones, footnote apparatus, trailer, dateline, and signature from printed pp. 66–82. It omits the article's German introduction and editorial discussion on pp. 57–65.

entities.label: Julius Zupitza, Jak and his step dame (1893)
alternate_labels: Iak and his step dame nach der Handschrift Rawlinson C 86 mit den Abweichungen des Porkington-Manuskripts
entities.description: Zupitza's German-language article and edition. Printed pp. 57–65 introduce the textual tradition and editorial method; pp. 66–82 print the R text with P variants known through Halliwell. The article explains the milestone sigla later retained in the XML and states that three missing lines in stanza LIX were supplied from P.

bibliographic.citation: Julius Zupitza, 'Jak and his step dame nach der Handschrift Rawlinson C 86 mit den Abweichungen des Porkington-Manuskripts,' Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Litteraturen 90 (1893), 57–82.
bibliographic.title: Jak and his step dame nach der Handschrift Rawlinson C 86 mit den Abweichungen des Porkington-Manuskripts
bibliographic.container_title: Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Litteraturen

notes: The article title is printed with I/J typography that is commonly normalized as 'Jak'; the XML's closing trailer reads 'Iak.'
The U-M encoding begins at the first stanza on p. 66 and ends with Zupitza's signature on p. 82.

entities.label: J. O. Halliwell, Early English Miscellanies (1855), The Friar and the Boy
entities.description: Halliwell's transcription of ten selections from the then privately held Porkington manuscript. The poem printed without a supplied verbal title beneath item number VII on pp. 46–62 transcribes the witness now cataloged as NLW Brogyntyn MS II.1, fols. 139r–150r. Halliwell's preface says the manuscript was temporarily entrusted to him for transcription.

bibliographic.citation: J. O. Halliwell, ed., Early English Miscellanies, in Prose and Verse, Selected from an Inedited Manuscript of the Fifteenth Century (London: Printed for the Warton Club, 1855), 46–62.
bibliographic.title: Early English Miscellanies, in Prose and Verse, Selected from an Inedited Manuscript of the Fifteenth Century

physical_description.extent: viii, 96 printed pages; the poem occupies pp. 46–62
notes: The next numbered selection, VIII, begins later on printed p. 62.

entities.label: Melissa M. Furrow, Ten Bourdes, Jack and His Stepdame
entities.description: The current Middle English Texts Series student edition and scholarly introduction. It is based primarily on R, provides a modern edited text, glosses, explanatory and textual notes, and a survey of the poem's manuscript and print transmission. It is supporting scholarship, not a source from which CME00073 was encoded.

bibliographic.citation: Melissa M. Furrow, ed., Ten Bourdes, Middle English Texts Series (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, Western Michigan University), especially 'Jack and His Stepdame,' 19–43.
bibliographic.title: Ten Bourdes
bibliographic.publisher: Medieval Institute Publications, Western Michigan University

physical_description.extent: complete downloadable edition, 276 PDF pages; Jack and His Stepdame introduction pp. 19–26, text pp. 27–41, textual notes pp. 42–43
physical_description.format: Born-digital scholarly edition and teaching text

entities.label: Bodleian Library, MS. Rawl. C. 86
alternate_labels: Oxford, Bodleian Libraries, MS. Rawl. C. 86
entities.description: The direct copy-text witness for Zupitza's edition. The Tale of Jack and His Stepdame occupies fols. 52r–59r in Part II of a composite miscellany. Fol. 52 is a replacement leaf; modern scholarship distinguishes the replacement hand from the principal scribe rather than treating the whole item as a single homogeneous copying event.

date_statements.label: Zupitza's dating of the poem's hands
date_statements.notes: The current catalog dates the composite and its parts separately across a wider range; this statement is retained specifically as Zupitza's assessment of the poem's two hands.

holding.notes: Held by the Bodleian Library, University of Oxford.

physical_description.support: paper in Part II; the composite codex as a whole contains mixed materials
physical_description.extent: ii later-paper leaves + 189 + i later-paper leaf, with one inserted unfoliated later-paper sheet; work at ff. 52r–59r
notes: The complete Digital Bodleian sequence includes boards, pastedowns, endleaves, the inserted flyleaf, all foliated leaves, binding edges, spine, head, and tail.

The current aggregate catalog dates the composite from the middle of the fifteenth century through the first half of the sixteenth; that range must not be collapsed into a precise date for this poem's copying.

entities.label: National Library of Wales, Brogyntyn MS II.1
entities.description: The comparative P witness, a Middle English miscellany written by sixteen scribes. The Friar and the Boy occupies ff. 139r–150r. The current official catalog calls the original restricted and directs readers to surrogates, while the Library also publishes a complete 425-canvas IIIF sequence.

holding.notes: Deposited in 1938 and purchased for the nation in 1993.

physical_description.dimensions: 140 × 105 mm; written space varies by quire
physical_description.format: Composite manuscript miscellany in a nineteenth-century half-calf binding
notes: The current catalog identifies sixteen scribes and dates the miscellany circa 1470.
The work ends on f. 150r, where the following item also begins.

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Archiv volume 90 (1893)
entities.description: A complete 500-page provider PDF of the journal volume containing Zupitza's article, retaining the binding, title matter, the full volume, adjacent articles, and terminal matter. The local copy is the complete source object, not a cropped article extract.

notes: The volume title page establishes 1893; inherited serial metadata that says 1846 is not the volume date.

entities.reproduction_format: PDF page images with embedded text and scan metadata

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Early English Miscellanies (1855)
entities.description: A complete 116-page provider PDF of Halliwell's book, preserving the binding and preliminaries as well as the complete edited collection. The Friar and the Boy occupies printed pp. 46–62, provider PDF pp. 60–76.
entities.reproduction_format: PDF page images with provider OCR

entities.label: Digital Bodleian complete IIIF sequence for MS. Rawl. C. 86
entities.description: The complete current provider-published 396-canvas color sequence, from the upper board through binding surfaces and every manuscript leaf to the tail. The work occupies fifteen surfaces, ff. 52r–59r, zero-based canvases 110–124.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Image API JPEG derivatives with bundled manifest and inventory

entities.label: National Library of Wales complete published IIIF sequence for Brogyntyn MS II.1
entities.description: The complete current 425-canvas provider-published sequence, from the front cover through f. 211v to the back cover. It covers the complete textual codex as published, including the work at ff. 139r–150r, but does not separately expose the spine, edges, pastedowns, inside covers, or every catalog-described flyleaf surface.

notes: The sequence labels the front cover, f. iii recto, ff. 1r–211v, and the back cover.

entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Image API JPEG derivatives with bundled manifest and inventory

entities.label: Bodleian Medieval Manuscripts catalog description for MS. Rawl. C. 86
entities.description: The current institutional description of the composite miscellany, its parts, contents, extent, binding, provenance, and work locus at ff. 52r–59r.

entities.label: National Library of Wales catalog and detailed contents for Brogyntyn MS II.1
entities.description: The current repository record and linked detailed contents handlist, which crosswalk former Porkington MS 10 to Brogyntyn MS II.1 and locate The Friar and the Boy at ff. 139r–150r.

rights.component: Repository XML file and repository-specific markup or additions
rights.basis: The file contains public-domain medieval and 1893 source text, but no file-specific license or complete authorship statement for repository-level XML work was found.
notes: Do not infer a repository-file license solely from the status of the encoded sources.

rights.component: U-M electronic text as presented in the Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse
rights.basis: The U-M collection states that its materials are in the public domain in the United States and displays the No Copyright–United States rights statement.

rights.component: Zupitza's 1893 article, transcription, apparatus, commentary, and typographic presentation
rights.jurisdiction: United States and jurisdictions with ordinary author-term copyright limits
rights.basis: The article was published in 1893 and its author died in 1895, placing it beyond ordinary publication- and author-term copyright limits.

rights.component: Halliwell's 1855 transcription, preface, edited collection, and typographic presentation
rights.jurisdiction: United States and jurisdictions with ordinary author-term copyright limits
rights.basis: The book was published in 1855 and its editor died in 1889, placing it beyond ordinary publication- and author-term copyright limits.

rights.component: Modern METS introduction, edited text, glosses, explanatory and textual notes, and downloadable PDF presentation
rights.basis: METS expressly states that its print and digital texts are copyrighted, that digital editions are provided for individual use only, and that reprinting requires permission. Its CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license is specifically limited to XML and is not generalized here to the PDF.
notes: The medieval underlying text is public domain, but that status does not remove rights in Furrow's modern introduction, editorial work, notes, glosses, or publication design.
Keep the complete PDF gitignored for local research and link users to the provider unless separate reuse permission or license is verified.

rights.component: medieval Middle English textual content of The Tale of Jack and His Stepdame
rights.jurisdiction: United States and jurisdictions with ordinary author-term copyright limits
rights.basis: The work and witness are medieval, many centuries beyond ordinary copyright terms. This does not determine rights in modern photography, descriptions, editions, or encodings.

rights.component: medieval Middle English textual content of The Friar and the Boy
rights.jurisdiction: United States and jurisdictions with ordinary author-term copyright limits
rights.basis: The work and circa-1470 witness are medieval, many centuries beyond ordinary copyright terms. This does not determine rights in modern photography, descriptions, editions, or encodings.

rights.component: The exact Internet Archive volume PDF mirrored on Wikimedia Commons
rights.basis: Wikimedia Commons marks the exact mirrored PDF with the Public Domain Mark; the source volume is independently public domain.

rights.component: The exact Internet Archive book PDF mirrored on Wikimedia Commons
rights.basis: Wikimedia Commons marks the exact mirrored PDF with the Public Domain Mark; the source book is independently public domain.

rights.component: Digital Bodleian manuscript photographs and the complete locally cached provider sequence
rights.basis: The item-specific IIIF attribution states Photo © Bodleian Libraries and makes the images available under Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 International, subject to Digital Bodleian's terms.
notes: Attribution and noncommercial conditions apply. The license does not authorize commercial or for-profit print-on-demand image use.
Keep the bundle gitignored. Publishing or commercially reusing it requires compliance with the license and, when applicable, additional permission from Bodleian Imaging Services.

rights.component: NLW digital manuscript images and the complete locally cached provider sequence
rights.basis: The item-specific IIIF manifest applies the Public Domain Mark. NLW's current explanation says marked works may be copied, modified, distributed, and performed, including commercially, without permission; attribution and related use guidance are expressly non-binding.
notes: Preserve the Public Domain Mark and credit the National Library of Wales as provider even though the Library describes its credit and other use guidelines as non-binding.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Zupitza's complete article on printed pp. 57–82
editorial_practices.representation: The poem, headings, apparatus, milestones, trailer, dateline, and signature on pp. 66–82 are encoded; introductory and editorial discussion on pp. 57–65 is omitted.
editorial_practices.description: The electronic text is an edited-text selection rather than an encoding of the complete article.

editorial_practices.representation: expanded letters are set in italic type and retained as HI REND=italic in the XML
editorial_practices.description: Zupitza states that italic type identifies his expansions of manuscript abbreviations.
Zupitza regularized capitalization rather than reproducing manuscript capitalization diplomatically.

editorial_practices.representation: editorially supplied punctuation and occasional hyphens
editorial_practices.description: Zupitza supplied punctuation and used occasional hyphens; the XML preserves this edited presentation rather than a diplomatic manuscript layout.

editorial_practices.source_feature: R copy text compared with P through Halliwell's transcription
editorial_practices.representation: selected P variants in footnotes, Halliwell page milestones, and three P-supplied lines in stanza LIX
editorial_practices.description: Zupitza reports substantive P variants but usually omits mere formal variants. P was not inspected directly.
editorial_practices.scope: edited text and apparatus on pp. 66–82

editorial_practices.source_feature: scribal flourishes, apparent R errors, and variant forms in P
editorial_practices.representation: flourishes ignored; obvious R errors corrected only rarely with readings noted; formal P variants generally omitted
editorial_practices.description: The edition is semi-normalized and selectively critical, not a complete graphic or variant transcription of either witness.
editorial_practices.scope: edited text and apparatus on pp. 66–82

evidence.citation: Kyvero-Press/CME, CME00073.xml at commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c
evidence.locator: TEMPHEAD/REVDESCR; IDG; TEXT/@LANG; BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='poem']; PB, LG, L, MILESTONE, NOTE, TRAILER, CLOSER
evidence.summary: Direct evidence for all three CME00073 identifiers, the 2016 revision, Middle English language declaration, poem boundary, printed pages 66–82, R folios 52r–59r, Halliwell pages 46–62, Hazlitt milestones, 73 top-level stanzas, 438 top-level lines, P apparatus, trailer, dateline, signature, and repository checksum.

evidence.citation: General Corpus structural inspection of CME00073
evidence.locator: whole XML tree and direct-child counts
evidence.summary: Verifies 59,749 bytes; one DIV1, 17 page breaks, 110 milestones, 340 notes, 73 top-level LG groups and 438 top-level L elements. One additional LG with twelve L elements is nested in a P-only final-stanza note, explaining the raw totals of 74 and 450.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, CME00073
evidence.summary: Confirms the stable U-M item identity, Zupitza attribution, 1893 publication information, public electronic-text access, and No Copyright–United States assertion.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, About the Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse
evidence.summary: States that later CME batches were manually keyed from public-domain editions, proofread and edited in house, and marked up with basic TEI semantics; the collection asserts No Copyright–United States.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan, CME source inventory with URLs
evidence.summary: Identifies CME00073 as Zupitza's Iak and his step dame from Archiv, while giving the discrepant year 1895. The year is corrected to 1893 from the primary volume and article rather than silently repeated.

evidence.citation: Julius Zupitza, 'Jak and his step dame nach der Handschrift Rawlinson C 86 mit den Abweichungen des Porkington-Manuskripts,' Archiv 90 (1893), 57–82
evidence.locator: article pp. 57–82; especially pp. 57–65 and edited text pp. 66–82
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for exact citation and date, R fols. 52r–59r, Zupitza's direct use of R, his statement that he knew P only through Halliwell, P-supplied lines, editorial normalization and expansion practices, collation selectivity, milestone sigla, and the exact text encoded by U-M.

evidence.citation: Local audit of the complete Archiv volume 90 provider PDF
evidence.locator: 500-page PDF; article printed pp. 57–82; CME text pp. 66–82
evidence.summary: Confirms a readable complete 47,236,061-byte provider PDF, 500 PDF pages, whole-volume coverage, article and CME page ranges, and exact SHA-256 checksum.

evidence.citation: J. O. Halliwell, Early English Miscellanies, in Prose and Verse (London: Warton Club, 1855)
evidence.locator: preface pp. iii–viii; item VII pp. 46–62
evidence.summary: Primary evidence that Halliwell received the Porkington manuscript for transcription and printed this poem on pp. 46–62. Visual inspection confirms the work begins under item VII on p. 46 and ends before item VIII on p. 62.

evidence.citation: Local audit of the complete Early English Miscellanies provider PDF
evidence.locator: 116-page PDF; work at printed pp. 46–62, provider PDF pp. 60–76
evidence.summary: Confirms a readable complete 4,406,439-byte provider PDF, whole-book coverage, binding and preliminaries, exact work range, embedded OCR, and SHA-256 checksum.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library catalog, Early English miscellanies
evidence.summary: Confirms Halliwell's name, title, London Warton Club imprint, 1855 date, viii + 96-page printed extent, series, and the inclusion of ten Porkington selections including The Friar and the Boy.

evidence.citation: Melissa Furrow, Introduction to Jack and His Stepdame, Middle English Texts Series
evidence.locator: Manuscripts, Scribes, and Printed Texts; Reference Tools; Poet, Poetry, and Language
evidence.summary: Current scholarly synthesis of titles and versions, R and P loci, R replacement leaf and scribes, broader transmission, NIMEV 977, ATU 592, six-line stanza form, likely fifteenth-century composition, and probable Norfolk origin. It identifies Zupitza 1893 as an edition of R with reference to P.

open_questions.question: How should the wider manuscript, early-print, and later chapbook tradition be modeled without conflating distinct endings and revisions with the immediate R-plus-mediated-P source of CME00073?
next_steps: Treat the METS witness and early-print survey as a starting point for a separate full work-transmission graph.
Add only relations supported by direct edition or witness evidence, keeping the CME derivation path visually distinct.

open_questions.question: What is the best current date assignment for the two hands copying the poem in Rawlinson C. 86, including replacement fol. 52?
next_steps: Consult the full part-level Bodleian catalog data and the modern paleographical studies cited there.
Keep Zupitza's late-fifteenth-century assessment explicitly attributed until a stronger item-level statement is verified.
